topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The music swelled, a vibrant, overwhelming wave of joy washing over the crowded ballroom. I clutched my lukewarm champagne flute, the crystal feeling slick and cold against my palm. Then I saw him. He was standing near the entrance, laughing, his familiar smile catching the light in a way that made my stomach clench. It was Mark. My ex. He looked… happy. A genuine, uncomplicated happiness that felt like a physical blow. My breath hitched, a shallow, panicked intake of air. I watched him move, the way his arm draped casually around his new partner, and a strange, hollow ache settled deep in my chest. I wanted to melt into the wallpaper, to become invisible. My eyes darted away, searching for an escape route, a shadow to hide behind. Every nerve ending in my body was screaming, a silent alarm bell signaling profound instability. I felt suddenly unsteady on my feet, as if the floor beneath me might give way.

[story 2]

“Oh, look who it is!” Sarah chirped, pulling me toward the dance floor. I forced a smile, a brittle thing that didn't reach my eyes. It was Mark. He looked exactly the same, maybe a little more polished, radiating an easy confidence that felt utterly unattainable. My hands started to tremble almost immediately, the slight vibration making the silverware on my plate rattle. I tried to focus on the intricate patterns on the rug, anything but the sight of him. When he glanced my way, a flicker—was it recognition? A shared memory? My throat tightened, making it difficult to swallow. I retreated to the edge of the crowd, leaning against a pillar, trying to anchor myself. My gaze kept snapping back to him, a desperate, involuntary pull, as if his presence was the only thing keeping my own equilibrium from completely collapsing. I needed to be near something solid, anything, to stop the internal tremor that was escalating into a full-body shake.

[story 3]

I was supposed to be circulating, making small talk, but the noise of the celebration had dissolved into a dull roar in my ears. Then, across the room, he materialized. Mark. He was talking to someone, his posture relaxed, completely at ease. A sudden, sharp constriction seized my chest, stealing the air I’d just managed to draw in. My fingers tightened around my glass until my knuckles were white. I watched him laugh, and the sound seemed amplified, echoing the emptiness inside me. A wave of profound vulnerability washed over me, stripping away any pretense of composure. I felt a strange, urgent need to be close to him, to somehow absorb some of his stability, to borrow his sense of groundedness. I took a slow, deliberate step forward, my legs feeling strangely heavy, as if moving required immense, unseen effort. My whole being seemed keyed up, waiting for a signal, a confirmation that I still mattered, even in this peripheral, observed space.
